reference: Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, 8th Edition, Kenneth H. Rosen, Chapter 6 (Sections 6.1–6.5), Pages 405–456

formulas.name: Product Rule
formulas.whenToUse: Task done in m sequential independent stages.

terms.means: number of choices available at step i

formulas.trap: If choices shrink each step (no repetition), use n, n−1, n−2, … — still multiply.

formulas.name: Sum Rule
formulas.whenToUse: Task done in exactly ONE of m mutually exclusive ways.

terms.means: number of ways for alternative i

formulas.trap: Alternatives must be disjoint. If they overlap, use the Subtraction Rule.

formulas.name: Subtraction Rule (Inclusion–Exclusion, 2 sets)
formulas.whenToUse: Two overlapping alternatives — the overlap is double-counted and must be removed.

terms.means: union — elements in A₁ or A₂ or both
intersection — elements in both A₁ and A₂

formulas.trap: Complementary counting is often faster: |A| = |U| − |Ā|. Prefer it when the complement is simpler.

formulas.name: Complement Rule (Complementary Counting)
formulas.whenToUse: Counting the 'good'/'at least one' outcomes directly is messy; count the whole universe U and subtract the easy complement (the 'none'/'bad' cases).

terms.means: universe - every outcome with no restriction
complement of A - the outcomes you do NOT want (usually far easier to count)

formulas.trap: Distinct from 2-set Inclusion-Exclusion |A1 u A2| = |A1| + |A2| - |A1 n A2|. The complement rule is the right tool for 'at least one' when the direct count overlaps messily.

formulas.name: Division Rule
formulas.whenToUse: Counting procedure generates every distinct outcome exactly d times.

terms.means: total count produced by the procedure
number of times each distinct outcome is counted

formulas.trap: C(n,r) = P(n,r) / r! is exactly this rule: each r-combination generates r! permutations.

formulas.name: Derangement (no element in its own position)
formulas.whenToUse: Arrange n items so that NO item lands in its own original position (e.g. no letter in its own envelope, no ball in its matching cell).

terms.means: number of arrangements with no item in its own spot

formulas.trap: Derived from Inclusion–Exclusion: start with n!, subtract arrangements fixing ≥1 item, add back overlaps, etc. Small values: D1=0, D2=1, D3=2, D4=9, D5=44. If some items are identical (e.g. two equal letters), the plain D_n overcounts — adjust for the repeats.

formulas.name: Pigeonhole Principle
formulas.latex: k+1 \text{ objects into } k \text{ boxes} \;\Rightarrow\; \exists\text{ box with} \geq 2 \text{ objects}
formulas.plain: k+1 objects into k boxes ⟹ some box has ≥ 2 objects
formulas.whenToUse: Prove that two items must share a category when objects outnumber categories.

terms.means: minimum objects to guarantee a collision

formulas.trap: Identify what are the 'objects' and what are the 'boxes' — that identification is the entire solution.

formulas.name: Generalized Pigeonhole Principle
formulas.latex: N \text{ objects into } k \text{ boxes} \;\Rightarrow\; \exists\text{ box with} \geq \left\lceil \frac{N}{k} \right\rceil \text{ objects}
formulas.plain: N objects into k boxes ⟹ some box has ≥ ⌈N/k⌉ objects
formulas.whenToUse: Find the guaranteed minimum maximum load when N objects fill k boxes.

terms.means: ceiling function — rounds up to nearest integer

formulas.name: Minimum N to Guarantee r Objects in One Box
formulas.whenToUse: Find the fewest objects needed so that some box is guaranteed to hold ≥ r objects.

terms.means: desired minimum number of objects in some box

formulas.trap: With k(r−1) objects you can put exactly r−1 in each box — no box has r yet. Must add 1 more.

formulas.name: Factorial
formulas.whenToUse: Count all arrangements of n distinct objects in a line.

terms.means: total ordered arrangements of all n objects

formulas.name: r-Permutation (no repetition)
formulas.whenToUse: Ordered selection of r distinct elements from n — order matters, no reuse.

terms.means: number of ordered r-selections from n

formulas.trap: P(n,n) = n! — arranging all n elements.

formulas.name: r-Combination (no repetition)
formulas.whenToUse: Unordered selection of r distinct elements from n — order does not matter, no reuse.

terms.means: orderings of the r chosen elements — divided out because order is irrelevant

formulas.trap: C(n,0) = C(n,n) = 1. When r is large, compute C(n, n−r) instead — same value, less arithmetic.

formulas.name: Symmetry Identity
formulas.whenToUse: Choosing r items is equivalent to choosing which n−r items to leave out.

formulas.name: Number of Subsets of an n-Element Set
formulas.whenToUse: Count all subsets (including ∅ and S itself) of a set with n elements.

terms.means: number of elements in the set S
power set — the set of all subsets of S
total subsets; each element is either in or out (2 choices × n times)

formulas.reference: Section 6.1.2, Example 10, p. 408; also from Corollary 1 of Binomial Theorem, p. 439

formulas.name: Total Functions from an m-Element Set to an n-Element Set
formulas.whenToUse: Count all functions f : A → B where |A| = m, |B| = n.

formulas.name: One-to-One (Injective) Functions from an m-Element Set to an n-Element Set
formulas.whenToUse: Count all injections f : A → B where |A| = m ≤ |B| = n.

terms.means: m-permutations of n; equals 0 when m > n (no injections exist)

formulas.trap: When m > n, no injection exists — the Pigeonhole Principle guarantees a collision.

formulas.name: Number of Even Permutations of n Objects
formulas.whenToUse: Count the even permutations of n objects (those reachable by an even number of swaps). Exactly half of all n! permutations are even.

terms.means: even permutations — equal in count to the odd permutations

formulas.trap: Holds only for n ≥ 2. For n = 1 there is just 1 permutation (the identity), which is even.
formulas.reference: Standard result (alternating group A_n has order n!/2)

formulas.name: r-Permutation with Repetition
formulas.whenToUse: Ordered selection of r items from n types — order matters, reuse allowed.

terms.means: number of types (elements that can be reused)

formulas.trap: Same formula as total functions (n^m). Context differs: here r is sequence length, n is alphabet size.

formulas.name: r-Combination with Repetition (Stars and Bars)
formulas.whenToUse: Unordered selection of r items from n types — order doesn't matter, reuse allowed.

terms.means: number of distinct types to choose from

formulas.trap: From Table 1 in §6.5: formula is C(n+r−1, r), not C(n+r, r). Off-by-one is the classic error.

formulas.name: Non-Negative Integer Solutions to x₁ + x₂ + ⋯ + xₙ = r
formulas.latex: x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_n = r \;\Longrightarrow\; C(n+r-1,\,r) \text{ solutions}
formulas.plain: x₁ + x₂ + ⋯ + xₙ = r  ⟹  C(n+r−1, r) non-negative integer solutions
formulas.whenToUse: Count integer solutions where each variable xᵢ ≥ 0.

terms.means: each variable is a non-negative integer

formulas.trap: If xᵢ ≥ cᵢ (lower bounds), substitute yᵢ = xᵢ − cᵢ first; the new total becomes r − Σcᵢ.

formulas.name: Multinomial Coefficient (Permutations with Indistinguishable Objects)
formulas.latex: \frac{n!}{n_1!\, n_2!\, \cdots\, n_k!}, \qquad n_1 + n_2 + \cdots + n_k = n
formulas.plain: n! / (n₁! · n₂! · ⋯ · nₖ!)  where  n₁ + n₂ + ⋯ + nₖ = n
formulas.whenToUse: Distinct arrangements of n objects where nᵢ objects of type i are identical.

terms.means: count of identical objects of type i

formulas.trap: All nᵢ must sum to n. Forgetting one type's factorial in the denominator overcounts.

formulas.name: Distributing n Distinguishable Objects into k Distinguishable Boxes
formulas.whenToUse: Objects are labeled (distinct), boxes are labeled (distinct), nᵢ objects go into box i.

terms.means: number of objects placed in box i
number of distinguishable boxes; n₁+n₂+⋯+nₖ = n

formulas.name: Distributing n Distinguishable Objects into k Indistinguishable Boxes
formulas.plain: Σ S(n,j)  for j = 1 to k  [no simple closed form]
formulas.whenToUse: Objects are labeled, boxes are identical (unlabeled) — use Stirling numbers.

terms.means: Stirling number of the second kind: ways to partition n labeled objects into j non-empty identical subsets
number of indistinguishable boxes (at most k may be non-empty)

formulas.trap: No closed-form formula exists. For GATE, expect small n where direct enumeration is feasible.

formulas.name: Distributing r Indistinguishable Objects into n Distinguishable Boxes
formulas.whenToUse: Objects are identical (unlabeled), boxes are labeled — each box may hold any number.
formulas.trap: Same formula as r-combinations with repetition. n and r play different roles here: n = boxes, r = objects.

formulas.name: Distributing r Indistinguishable Objects into k Indistinguishable Boxes
formulas.latex: p_k(r) \;=\; \text{partitions of } r \text{ into} \leq k \text{ positive parts}
formulas.plain: pₖ(r) — number of integer partitions of r into at most k parts [no closed form]
formulas.whenToUse: Both objects and boxes are identical — equivalent to writing r as an unordered sum.

terms.means: number of integer partitions of r into at most k positive parts

formulas.trap: No closed formula. Example: 6 identical books into 4 identical boxes has only 9 ways (enumerated directly).

formulas.name: Binomial Theorem
formulas.plain: (x+y)ⁿ = Σ C(n,j) xⁿ⁻ʲ yʲ   (j = 0 to n)
formulas.whenToUse: Expand a power of a two-term sum; find the coefficient of a specific term.

terms.means: binomial coefficient — coefficient of x^(n−j) y^j

formulas.trap: For (ax + by)^n, apply the theorem to (ax + by)^n directly: coefficient of x^k y^(n−k) is C(n,k)·aᵏ·b^(n−k).

formulas.name: Sum of Binomial Coefficients = 2ⁿ
formulas.whenToUse: Sum of all entries in row n of Pascal's Triangle.

terms.means: total number of subsets of an n-element set

formulas.name: Alternating Sum of Binomial Coefficients = 0
formulas.whenToUse: Alternating-sign sum of row n (n ≥ 1). Equivalently: even-indexed entries sum = odd-indexed entries sum.

terms.means: alternating sign: +1 for even k, −1 for odd k

formulas.name: Weighted Sum of Binomial Coefficients = 3ⁿ
formulas.whenToUse: Evaluate a sum of binomial coefficients each weighted by a power of 2.

terms.means: weight applied to each coefficient (from Binomial Theorem with x=1, y=2)

formulas.name: Pascal's Identity
formulas.whenToUse: Compute C(n+1,k) recursively, or prove combinatorial identities. Basis of Pascal's Triangle.

terms.means: entry in the (n+1)th row — equals sum of two entries above it

formulas.trap: Initial conditions: C(n,0) = C(n,n) = 1 for all n ≥ 0.

formulas.name: Vandermonde's Identity
formulas.plain: C(m+n, r) = Σ C(m, r−k)·C(n, k)  (k = 0 to r)
formulas.whenToUse: Selecting r elements from two disjoint groups of sizes m and n.

terms.means: total elements chosen (r ≤ m and r ≤ n)
number chosen from the second set; r−k chosen from the first

formulas.name: Corollary to Vandermonde's Identity
formulas.whenToUse: Evaluate a sum of squared binomial coefficients.

terms.means: square of the kth binomial coefficient in row n
central binomial coefficient — middle entry of row 2n

formulas.name: Hockey Stick Identity (Theorem 4)
formulas.plain: Σ C(j,r) = C(n+1, r+1)  (j = r to n)
formulas.whenToUse: Evaluate a sum of binomial coefficients with fixed lower index r and varying upper index.

terms.means: upper limit of the sum; n ≥ r
summation index from r to n

formulas.name: Catalan Number
formulas.whenToUse: Count balanced/non-crossing structures: rooted binary trees with n nodes, valid parenthesisations of n+1 factors (matrix-chain), Dyck paths / binary strings where every prefix has at least as many 0's as 1's, balanced bracket sequences.

terms.means: size parameter (nodes, pairs of brackets, up/down steps, etc.)
central binomial coefficient — all balanced sequences ignoring the prefix rule
the factor that removes sequences violating the never-go-below rule

formulas.trap: C(2n,n) alone counts ALL balanced strings; the extra 1/(n+1) factor enforces the prefix/never-negative condition. Small values: C0=1, C1=1, C2=2, C3=5, C4=14, C5=42.

formulas.name: Telescoping Series
formulas.plain: Σ (f(x) − f(x+1)) = f(1) − f(m+1)   — middle terms cancel
formulas.whenToUse: Sum a series whose general term can be split into a difference of consecutive pieces, e.g. 1/(x(x+1)) = 1/x − 1/(x+1). Most middle terms cancel.

terms.means: the piece such that each term equals f(x) − f(x+1)

formulas.trap: Key split: 1/(x(x+1)) = 1/x − 1/(x+1). After cancellation only the first and last pieces remain. NOTE: this is an algebra/series technique, not a counting formula — included because a PYQ needs it.
formulas.reference: Algebra (series); outside the core counting syllabus

formulas.name: Fermat's Little Theorem
formulas.plain: a^(p−1) ≡ 1 (mod p)   when p is prime and p does not divide a
formulas.whenToUse: Compute a^huge mod p quickly when p is prime: reduce the exponent modulo (p−1), then handle the small leftover power.

terms.means: the exponent cycle length; reduce big exponents mod (p−1)

formulas.trap: Write the exponent as (p−1)·q + r. Then a^exp ≡ (a^(p−1))^q · a^r ≡ 1^q · a^r ≡ a^r (mod p). Requires p prime AND p∤a. NOTE: this is number theory, not counting — included because two PYQs need it.
formulas.reference: Number theory (Rosen §4.4, Fermat's Little Theorem); outside the core counting syllabus
